context: Current closing price of Starbucks (SBUX): $99.54 USD as of 2026-04-23.
justification_text:
My point estimates reflect a mild mean-reversion assumption anchored near the current price level. At the one-day horizon, I placed the estimate at 99.8, essentially flat with a slight downward drift, because intraday momentum and market microstructure tend to dominate at short horizons and there was no strong directional signal to justify a meaningful move. At the one-week horizon, I nudged the estimate up slightly to 100.5, reflecting a modest positive drift assumption consistent with the long-run tendency of risk assets to appreciate over time, while acknowledging that a week is long enough for macro catalysts to materialize. At the one-month horizon, the estimate rises marginally to 101.0, again reflecting that same long-run drift premium, though the incremental increase is small because I did not have strong conviction in any particular directional catalyst over that window.

The confidence interval widths are driven primarily by realized and implied volatility scaled to each time horizon. Daily volatility for most liquid assets tends to be in the range of 1–2%, which maps reasonably onto the roughly ±3.3-point spread I assigned for the one-day interval. As the horizon extends, uncertainty compounds roughly with the square root of time, which is why the one-week interval widens to approximately ±7.5 points and the one-month interval expands further to ±15 points. Liquidity conditions, bid-ask dynamics, and the general regime of volatility at the time of the forecast all fed into calibrating these widths.

Beyond statistical volatility, there are several structural risks that could push outcomes well outside these stated intervals. On the downside, a sudden macroeconomic shock — such as an unexpected central bank policy shift, a geopolitical escalation, or a credit event in a systemically important institution — could produce moves far exceeding two standard deviations, especially at the one-week and one-month horizons. On the upside, a surprise positive catalyst such as a major policy easing announcement, a strong earnings or economic data beat, or a resolution of an ongoing geopolitical tension could similarly produce outsized gains.

It is worth being explicit that my intervals are not designed to capture tail events with high probability — a 90% confidence interval by construction excludes roughly one in ten outcomes, and in financial markets those excluded outcomes are often fat-tailed rather than normally distributed. This means the true probability of extreme moves beyond my stated bounds is somewhat higher than the 10% residual would imply under a Gaussian assumption. Anyone relying on these estimates should treat them as a central tendency guide rather than a hard bound on possible outcomes.
